input.topic: A retired postal worker who has hand-drawn a detailed map of every street in the city over 40 years, and a local museum has acquired the collection.
input.angle: The maps are more accurate than digital mapping for certain historical details that were demolished or renamed.
input.interview_notes: He started in 1984 with a notebook. Has filled 47 notebooks. Each street drawn from memory after walking his route. Quote 1: 'Google can tell you where a street is. I can tell you where it was.' Quote 2: 'The city I mapped doesn't exist anymore. But the lines are still there in my notebooks.' The museum curator says the collection fills gaps in the city's architectural record. Three demolished buildings only appear in his notebooks. His wife says he has walked an estimated 80,000 miles.

input.topic: A community fridge programme that redistributes surplus restaurant food to residents.
input.angle: It started with one fridge and now operates 14 across the city.
input.interview_notes: Started by a restaurant owner who hated waste. Quote: 'We were throwing away enough food to feed 30 families a night.' Now collects from 40 restaurants. Volunteers manage pickup schedules.

input.topic: A small Austrian village that went from depopulation to a waiting list for housing after investing in ultra-fast broadband and marketing itself as a remote-work destination.
input.angle: Whether the model is replicable or whether it depends on unique local factors.
input.interview_notes: Village population: 340 in 2018, now 620. Mayor invested 1.2 million euros in fibre-optic. Quote from mayor: 'We had two choices: become a museum or become a laboratory.' 23 remote workers moved from Vienna and Munich. A co-working space opened in the old schoolhouse. Quote from a Viennese software developer: 'I pay half the rent and my commute is a five-minute walk through a forest.' But: the local school still has only one teacher. The nearest hospital is 45 minutes. Not everyone is welcome — a Bosnian family was refused by a landlord.

input.topic: Indian migrant construction workers in the Gulf states who send money home to build houses they have never lived in — the houses stand empty in Kerala and Tamil Nadu villages while the men work abroad for decades.
input.angle: The emotional and economic paradox of building a home you may never occupy, and what happens to the families left behind.
input.interview_notes: Focus on one worker, Suresh, who has been in Doha for 19 years. His house in Kerala has four bedrooms but no one sleeps in them — his wife and children live with her parents next door because the house feels too large and empty. Quote from Suresh (phone interview): 'I built the house so my children would know their father did something. But they grew up without me in it.' His wife: 'The house is beautiful. I clean it every week. But I do not cook there because cooking for one person in a big kitchen makes me sad.' The village has 40 such houses — locally called 'Gulf houses' — many half-finished because the worker ran out of contract renewals. A local bank manager estimates 60% of home loans in the district are financed by Gulf remittances. A sociologist who studies Gulf migration says the houses function as 'monuments to absence.' Suresh's eldest son, now 22, says he does not want to go to the Gulf: 'I watched my father build a house on a phone screen. I would rather rent and be present.'

input.topic: A whistleblower at a regional waste management company claims the firm has been dumping untreated industrial waste into a river for three years.
input.angle: The environmental and public health impact on downstream communities.
input.interview_notes: Whistleblower spoke on condition of anonymity. Provided two internal emails (unverified) showing schedule changes to avoid inspection periods. The company declined to comment. The environmental regulator said it is 'aware of the allegations' but would not confirm or deny an investigation. One downstream resident says the river water has changed colour in the past year but cannot say why. No independent water testing has been conducted. A local GP says she has seen an increase in skin complaints but has no data linking it to the river. The reporter has been unable to obtain official water quality data for the relevant stretch of river.

input.topic: A Nairobi-based architect who designs affordable housing using compressed earth blocks made from construction demolition waste, and her first 200-unit development is nearing completion on the outskirts of the city.
input.angle: Whether recycled-material construction can scale to meet East Africa's urban housing deficit, or whether regulatory and financing barriers will keep it marginal.
input.interview_notes: The architect, Faith Mwangi, left a corporate firm four years ago to start a materials research lab. She developed a compressed earth block that uses 40% demolition rubble and costs 35% less than conventional concrete blocks. Quote from Mwangi: 'Nairobi demolishes enough buildings every year to house 10,000 families. We are turning rubble into roofs.' The 200-unit development in Ruiru is priced at 1.8 million Kenyan shillings per unit — about 30% below comparable developments. 70% of units are pre-sold. But: commercial banks will not issue mortgages against non-conventional construction materials. Buyers must pay cash or use a cooperative savings scheme. A structural engineer who tested the blocks says they exceed national building code requirements for compressive strength. The county government is watching but has not endorsed the material in its housing policy. Quote from a housing policy researcher: 'The technology works. The question is whether the financial system and the regulators will let it work at scale.' One buyer, a teacher named James Odhiambo, says: 'I could never afford a concrete house. This is the first time owning a home felt possible.'

input.topic: A collective of women calligraphers in a Gulf state capital who are reviving traditional Arabic script styles that were disappearing, and have secured a commission to produce public art installations across the city's new metro stations.
input.angle: The intersection of cultural preservation, women's artistic leadership, and the tension between heritage and the rapid modernization of Gulf cities.
input.interview_notes: The collective, Khat Atelier, was founded three years ago by Mariam Al-Rashidi, a former graphic designer. It has 14 members, all women, ranging in age from 24 to 61. They practice Diwani, Thuluth, and Kufic scripts — styles that were historically transmitted master-to-apprentice but have few remaining practitioners. Quote from Al-Rashidi: 'The city is covered in glass and steel now. We want people to step off the metro and remember that this language is also architecture.' The metro commission covers 11 stations, each featuring a different historical text rendered in a script style connected to the region's literary history. The eldest member, Fatima, 61, learned Thuluth from her grandfather and says she had no one to teach until the collective formed: 'For thirty years I practised alone. Now I have students who are better than me.' A city cultural authority official says the metro project is the largest public calligraphy commission in the country's history. But: some members worry the commission is tokenistic — public art as decoration rather than sustained institutional support for the art form. Quote from a younger member, Noura, 27: 'I do not want to be a heritage exhibit. I want a salary and a studio and students of my own.' The collective currently has no permanent space and works from Al-Rashidi's living room.
